1: The altar of burnt offering (mizbach ha'olah) — 5 × 5 × 3 cubits of acacia wood. The bronze altar was the largest and most physically imposing object in the sanctuary compound, stationed at the entrance to the court. Its construction followed the blueprint of Exodus 27:1–8 exactly. The acacia wood, though eventually overlaid with bronze, had to withstand the thermal stresses of an outdoor fire altar; the hollow construction (Exodus 27:8) provided structural flexibility.
2: Four horns at the four corners formed from the altar itself — bronze-overlaid with all altar surfaces. The horns are the altar's most theologically charged features: blood of the sin offering was applied to the horns (Exodus 29:12); Aaron's sons carried the blood into the tent and applied it first to the incense altar's horns on Yom Kippur (Exodus 30:10). The four-directional horns of the bronze altar were the meeting-point of sacrifice and atonement in Israel's public worship.
3: All the altar's utensils — pots, shovels, basins, forks, fire-pans — all of bronze. The complete service set for the altar implemented Exodus 27:3. Bronze was the practical choice for objects exposed to fire, ash, blood, and weather: harder and more durable than any alternative available to ancient smiths, and resistant to the chemical effects of repeated high-temperature use.
4: A bronze grating of network (mikhbar ma'aseh resheth nechoshet) under the altar's ledge, reaching halfway up the 3-cubit height. The grating implemented Exodus 27:4–5. The bronze mesh platform served as the combustion surface — sacrificial animals and grain placed on the grating, ash falling below, airflow maintained from beneath. The network construction (interlaced bronze rods) provided strength while allowing the necessary air circulation for sustained combustion.
5: Four rings cast at the four corners of the bronze grating for the carrying poles. The rings on the grating (not on the altar body) meant the grating was the structural load-bearer for transport. When the Levites carried the altar, they lifted from the grating-rings, not from the altar's outer bronze surface. The engineering distributed the carrying load to the strongest structural element.
6: Acacia-wood poles overlaid with bronze — bronze rather than gold for the altar's poles, following the holiness-gradient. The altar's poles are the only carrying poles in the sanctuary not overlaid with gold. Bronze poles for the bronze altar, gold poles for the gold-interior objects — the material consistency extended even to the transport infrastructure.
7: The poles inserted through the grating-rings on the two sides of the altar for carrying. The carrying system implemented Exodus 27:6–7. The poles running through the grating rings meant the altar hung between the poles during transport — a suspension carry that balanced the hollow bronze structure without stressing any single point of the altar body.
8: The bronze laver (kiyyor nechoshet) and its bronze stand, made from the bronze mirrors (mar'ot hatzove'ot) of the serving women (hatzove'ot asher tzav'u*) at the entrance of the tent of meeting. This is one of the most striking verses in the construction account: the laver — the purification vessel where priests washed before serving — was made from mirrors donated by women who served at the sanctuary entrance. Their sacrificed beauty-tools became the purification source for all priestly ministry. The laver reflects both literally (it was made from mirrors) and metaphorically: the priest who washed in it saw himself reflected in the very material of the women's devotion.
9: The court hangings for the south side — 100 cubits of fine twisted linen with twenty pillars and twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks and bands. The court construction implemented Exodus 27:9–10. The fine linen of the court hangings (the same material as the innermost tent curtains) elevated the public space of the sanctuary: even Israel's approach route was curtained in royal-quality fabric. The boundary between the camp and the sacred compound was itself a threshold of beauty.
10: Their twenty pillars and twenty bronze sockets with silver hooks and bands on the pillars. The detailed material specification for the court pillars follows 27:10 exactly. The silver decorative elements on bronze-socketed pillars embodied the court's intermediate holiness grade — not as precious as the inner tent's gold-and-silver, but not common either. The visible silver (hooks and bands) marked the court pillars as sacred boundary-markers.
11: The north side: 100 cubits with twenty pillars, twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks and bands — identical to the south. The bilateral symmetry of the court's long walls created an enclosure that was consistent from all approach angles. Whether an Israelite approached the court from the east (the front entrance), circled to the south, or came from the north, the same pillar-with-silver-banding greeted them at the boundary of the sacred space.
12: The west end (50 cubits): ten pillars with ten sockets, silver hooks and bands. The shorter west wall maintained the same material standard as the long walls (silver hooks and bands, bronze sockets) while halving the pillar count proportionally to the shorter span. The west wall's backing position — facing away from the camp's approach — received the same workmanship as the more visible east and long sides.
13: The east end (50 cubits): the entrance side. Unlike the west end, the east is specified as the mizrach (sunrise side) — the front-facing side toward which the camp looked. The east orientation of the sanctuary entrance was not arbitrary: it placed Israel's approach toward the rising sun (and, in the exodus context, toward Egypt in the west). Approaching the sanctuary from the east was approaching from the direction of the created order's daily renewal.
14: Fifteen cubits of hangings on one side of the gate entrance (south flank) with three pillars and three sockets. The gate flanks implemented Exodus 27:14. The gate's south flank of fifteen cubits, with three pillars at five-cubit intervals, maintained the same spacing as the long walls. From the camp's approach, the gate opening was flanked by matching panels of fine linen, framing the entrance as a deliberate threshold rather than a gap in the enclosure.
15: The same on the other side (north flank): fifteen cubits, three pillars, three sockets. The symmetry of the gate flanks (15+15) with the twenty-cubit gate screen between them balanced the fifty-cubit east wall. The symmetric flanking panels made the gate a centered architectural feature — the east wall was not a wall with a door but an entrance design centered on an axis.
16: All the hangings around the court were of fine twisted linen. The material uniformity of the court's boundary — fine linen throughout — meant the entire sacred perimeter was of the same fabric as the innermost sanctuary walls. From outside, the court looked like a field of white linen (fine linen undyed was white); from inside, the priest serving at the altar was enclosed in the same material as the tabernacle. The court was not a lesser space but a continuous extension of the sanctuary's fabric.
17: The pillar sockets were bronze, the hooks and bands were silver, the pillar tops (tzippui rashehem*) gold-overlaid. This is the only verse describing gold on the court pillars — the gold-overlaid tops were not mentioned in the blueprint (Exodus 27:10–17), but their execution adds a detail: the topmost visible element of each court pillar was golden. The pillars thus embodied the full material hierarchy in a single object: bronze foot, silver body, gold crown — ground-level common, middle sacred, top most precious.
18: The gate screen of twenty cubits — blue, purple, crimson, and fine linen, the work of an embroiderer — with four pillars and four sockets of bronze, silver hooks and gold-overlaid tops. The gate screen's four sacred colors matched the tabernacle's entrance screen and inner veil, making the court entrance visually continuous with the inner sanctuary's threshold elements. The embroidered gate screen was the first sacred object an Israelite saw when approaching the sanctuary — it communicated that crossing this threshold meant entering YHWH's domain.
19: Their four pillars, four sockets of bronze, silver hooks, and silver bands on the tops. The gate screen's four pillars (vs. five for the tabernacle entrance, three per gate flank) were the public face of the sanctuary's most accessible threshold. The gate screen's pillar count (four) signals that this threshold, while the most public, was also the most liminal — four is the number of the earth's directions, and the gate was where all directions of Israel's camp converged.
20: All the pegs of the tabernacle and the court all around — bronze. The tent pegs that anchored the guy-ropes of the entire structure were all bronze. The pegs were the most unglamorous and most essential hardware of the sanctuary: driven into the desert ground, bearing the tension loads of the curtain walls and roof, invisible in use. Bronze's hardness made it ideal for ground penetration without buckling — the same practical logic that made bronze the altar metal.
21: This is the inventory (pekudei) of the tabernacle — the tabernacle of the testimony — as it was counted (poked*) at Moses's command, by the work of the Levites under the direction of Ithamar son of Aaron the priest. The sanctuary accounts were maintained by the Levites under priestly oversight — a formal accounting of all materials used. This inventory is the ancient world's equivalent of a post-construction audit: materials in, materials used, balance accounted. The tabernacle is perhaps the best-documented sacred construction project in ancient Near Eastern literature.
22: Bezalel son of Uri son of Hur of the tribe of Judah made all that YHWH commanded Moses. The summary of Bezalel's work gives his full genealogy — son, grandfather, tribe. The triple genealogy for the master craftsman elevates him to the same documentary status as a king or high priest in biblical genealogical practice. Bezalel's lineage is recorded because his vocation was as significant as any royal or priestly appointment in Israel's history.
23: Oholiab son of Ahisamach of the tribe of Dan — engraver, designer, embroiderer in blue, purple, and crimson and fine linen. Oholiab is the designer-decorator whose specific skills (engraving, artistic design, embroidery) complemented Bezalel's metalworking and construction skills. Dan and Judah together — the northern extreme and the royal southern tribe — built the sanctuary together. The tabernacle was not a Judahite project but an all-Israel work.
24: All the gold used for the work — all the wave-offering gold — 29 talents and 730 shekels by the sanctuary shekel. Approximately 1,000 kg of gold. This is one of the few ancient sanctuaries with surviving material cost documentation. The gold figure (29 talents + 730 shekels) was the total wave-offering gold — the voluntary contribution from Israel's post-exodus plunder. The entire gold economy of the tabernacle came from the freewill gifts of a newly freed people.
25: The silver from the community census: 100 talents and 1,775 shekels by the sanctuary shekel. The 100 talents of silver came from the census ransom-tax (Exodus 30:12–16): 603,550 males × half a shekel = 301,775 shekels = approximately 100 talents and 1,775 shekels. The census count embedded in the silver total confirms the population figure of Numbers 1: the silver material record and the census record cross-validate each other.
26: A beka per person — half a shekel by the sanctuary shekel — from everyone who crossed over into the counted, twenty years and older, 603,550 men. The beka-per-person rate (half a shekel = one beka) confirms that every counted male paid exactly the same amount. The silver foundation of the tabernacle thus preserved, in its very material, the record of every Israelite man who crossed from Egypt to Sinai: the sanctuary was built on the ransom of every named life.
27: 100 talents of silver for casting the 100 sockets of the sanctuary and the 100 sockets of the veil — one talent per socket. The one-talent-per-socket rate was precise: 100 sockets, 100 talents, one-to-one. The silver sockets were identical in weight, confirming that each was made to the same specification. The architectural foundation of YHWH's dwelling required exactly as much silver as was collected from the census ransom — a perfectly calibrated covenant economy.
28: 1,775 shekels of silver for the pillar hooks, gold-overlaid tops, and bands. The remaining silver after the socket-casting (1,775 shekels from the census surplus) was used for the visible decorative elements of the court pillars. The census surplus — the fraction of shekels above the 100 talents — was not wasted but applied to the court's visible beauty. Every shekel of ransom silver found its place in YHWH's dwelling.
29: The bronze wave-offering: 70 talents and 2,400 shekels. Approximately 2,400 kg of bronze — the largest single-metal quantity by weight in the tabernacle, concentrated in the outer court (altar, laver, sockets, pegs). The high bronze weight reflects the court's construction requirements: the altar alone (5 × 5 × 3 cubits of bronze-covered framework plus grating) required substantial metal, and the 96 court pillar sockets plus the tent pegs added significantly to the total.
30: The bronze used for: the entrance sockets of the tent of meeting, the bronze altar with its bronze grating, all the altar's utensils, the court sockets all around, the court gate sockets, and all the tabernacle pegs and court pegs. The comprehensive itemization of bronze applications closes the metal inventory with an exhaustive list: nothing was omitted, nothing unaccounted. The tabernacle's material transparency — full accounting of every metal in and its application — is itself a statement about the character of YHWH's economy: nothing is hidden, everything is accounted for.
31: The court pegs and all the pegs of the tabernacle surrounding the court. This final inventory line closes the construction accounts of chapters 36–38. The pegs — the most humble, most functional, least visible elements of the entire sanctuary — receive the same accounting as the ark and the lampstand. In YHWH's building, the pegs that held the tent in the desert wind mattered as much as the gold that covered the ark. Nothing in the covenant economy was too small to count.
